main_summary.summary: Carrie Lamar Young, interviewed September 26, 2011 at her home in Little Rock, Arkansas by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program, recounts her trajectory from sharecropping childhood in Lexa and Barton, Arkansas (Phillips County) to SNCC organizing in Helena and West Helena, Arkansas beginning in 1965. Born to Lazarus and Lottie Lamar, she describes Delta plantation labor, her mother's midwifery work, and the Lamar family's renaming from the Raymond plantation. She joined SNCC's Arkansas Project after Howard Himmelbaum, Bill Hansen, Jim Jones, and Jerry Casey arrived in West Helena, Arkansas, gathering signatures to abolish the poll tax, teaching literacy so people could register to vote, and integrating the Midland Heights bowling alley with Stanley Tillman before the Civil Rights Act passed. Young married Himmelbaum in March 1967, lived through interracial-marriage hostility, won class-action employment lawsuits against AMF Inc and National Old Line Life Insurance, helped elect Winthrop Rockefeller, co-founded the Black United Youth in Little Rock, and was in Memphis in 1968 supporting Dr. King. She later attended Malcolm X Liberation University in Durham in 1971.
key_themes: Poll tax abolition and voter education
Interracial marriage in the segregated South
Phillips County and the Elaine Massacre legacy
Black United Youth and post-SNCC activism
main_summary.historical_significance:
Young's interview is one of the few first-person accounts of SNCC's Arkansas Project from a Helena/West Helena, Arkansas-based local organizer rather than from one of the visiting field secretaries. She names specific Arkansas SNCC personnel (Howard Himmelbaum, Bill Hansen, Jim Jones, Jerry Casey, James Heel, Stanley Tillman, Michael Simmons, Myrtle Glasgow) and reconstructs the practical work of poll-tax-abolition signature drives and literacy classes that preceded the 1965 Voting Rights Act in the Arkansas Delta. Her account of Phillips County children walking on cracked earth and being told the hardness came from blood preserves an oral transmission of the 1919 Elaine Massacre memory to a generation born decades later. Her testimony on the founding of the Black United Youth in Little Rock, the 1968 desegregation confrontation in Earle, Arkansas, and the role of John W. Walker in Young v. Lozano provides specific organizational and legal history of post-SNCC Arkansas movement work.

The interview also documents the personal cost of interracial activist marriage in 1960s-70s Arkansas and Young's reflections, in 2011, on the limits she perceived in the movement's economic outcomes and on Barack Obama's election.

chapters.title: Family and Lamar Genealogy
chapters.summary: Young describes her parents Lazarus and Lottie Lamar, her mother's work as a midwife who could turn breach babies, and her father's literacy at a third-grade level with strong mathematical ability. She traces the Lamar family's name change from "Raymond" (their plantation name) made by her great-great-grandfather, and names her maternal uncles James and George Bell of Barton and West Helena, Arkansas, and her aunt Bessie Lipsy of LaGrange.
related_events: Post-Reconstruction family-name changes from plantation names

chapters.title: Sharecropping and Cotton Labor
chapters.summary: Young details the daily labor of sharecropping in Lexa, Arkansas, picking and chopping cotton from sun-up to sundown, raising hogs and cleaning chitterlings, canning over 300 jars of food per year. She names Homer Tiles, a white farmer who protected her from her mother's whippings at the cotton-weighing scale, and recalls earning $3.50 per day chopping cotton and around $3 per hundred pounds picked.

chapters.title: Church, School, and Jim Crow Awareness
chapters.summary: Young recalls First Baptist Church Lexa, Reverend Devaro, and superintendent Gus Wilburn, and her early exposure to gospel singing at revivals. She describes books with covers torn off and pages missing arriving from white schools, her mother's emphasis on reading despite her own inability to read or write, and an incident in which her father stood armed guard with other Black men to protect a young Black man falsely accused by a white merchant's daughter.
related_events: Local extralegal racial violence and Black community self-protection

chapters.title: Phillips County and the Elaine Massacre
chapters.summary: Young recalls being told as a teenager working the Phillips County fields that the unusual hardness of the cracked earth came from the blood in it, her introduction to the 1919 Elaine Massacre. She notes the NAACP had no visible presence in West Helena, Arkansas in her childhood, that news of the 1957 Little Rock Nine never reached her family, and that her home had no electricity until 1958.

chapters.title: SNCC Arrives in West Helena, Arkansas
chapters.summary: Young describes SNCC field organizers Howard Himmelbaum, Jim Jones, Bill Hansen, and Jerry Casey arriving in West Helena, Arkansas around 1965, holding meetings in local churches focused on voter education and poll tax abolition. She describes her father's fear of white retaliation and Molotov-cocktail attacks on civil rights workers' housing. She names Julian Bond, Andrew Young, Stokely Carmichael, Eldridge Cleaver, and Marion Barry as visitors to West Helena, Arkansas, and recounts her own work sitting on a storefront porch collecting poll-tax-abolition signatures.

chapters.title: Integrating the Bowling Alley
chapters.summary: Young recounts SNCC organizer Stanley Tillman taking her to the segregated Midland Heights bowling alley between Helena, Arkansas and West Helena, Arkansas before the Civil Rights Act passed. They were denied rental shoes but bowled in their socks until police arrived; her father's reputation got her sent home rather than arrested. Young's first bowling-game score was 98.

chapters.title: Building Relationships with the Organizers
chapters.summary: Young recounts how she came to know the SNCC organizers (most in their early twenties when she was about 15), the Ferndale, Arkansas workshops at a Presbyterian village, sit-ins at the Arkansas state capitol cafeteria where demonstrators were thrown out and mustard-gas-bombed, and the cotton-field assault by five young men at Snow Lake, Arkansas that left her hospitalized for three weeks. She also discusses the small mixed-race solidarity she experienced in Lexa from the Muscalino family and Homer Tiles.

chapters.title: Marriage to Howard Himmelbaum and Community Reaction
chapters.summary: Young describes her secret engagement to SNCC organizer Howard Himmelbaum, her departure for Washington, D.C. and Head Start training in May 1966, and their March 1967 wedding in New York. She recounts her parents' reactions, her mother loved Howard, her father feared for her safety, and the hostility she received from members of the Black community for marrying a white man, including a confrontation with her father's friend who made a derogatory comment as she walked to church.
related_events: Interracial marriages in the post-Loving v. Virginia South

chapters.title: SNCC Tensions and the Helena Judge
chapters.summary: Young recalls Judge Hester of Helena, Arkansas threatening James Heel in open court, telling the 21-year-old engineer with a master's degree to leave Helena, Arkansas "before you find yourself floating up." She describes the welcome SNCC organizers received from some Black pastors but not all, the freedom house in West Helena, Arkansas, Myrtle Glasgow's arrival from Oakland in 1964 and her becoming like an older sister to Young after the death of Young's biological sister. She also discusses the emerging tensions within SNCC between nonviolence and armed self-defense.
related_events: SNCC nonviolence vs. armed self-defense debate

chapters.title: Memphis 1968 and Cornell BLF
chapters.summary: Young describes traveling to Memphis in 1968 to hear and protect Dr. King, sharing rooms with Stokely Carmichael and Eldridge Cleaver. She recounts the rise of the Black Panther Party and the Cornell University Black Liberation Front armed-occupation photograph, including Ed Whitfield and "Eric" from Little Rock, Arkansas (the three young Cornell BLF members on the magazine cover). She argues armed-self-defense formations existed to protect leaders, not to attack white people.

chapters.title: OIC, Class-Action Lawsuits, and Rockefeller
chapters.summary: Young describes training at the Opportunities Industrialization Center as a key-punch operator, winning class-action discrimination lawsuits in Pulaski County against AMF Inc and National Old Line Life Insurance Company. She recounts being hired and then fired at the Arkansas Motor Vehicle Department by supervisor Lozano after he discovered she was married to a white man; attorney John W. Walker took the case to Governor Winthrop Rockefeller, who fired Lozano in the conference room. She credits the Black community's role in electing Rockefeller as Arkansas's first Republican governor of the era.
related_events: Young v. Lozano employment discrimination case

chapters.title: Black United Youth and Earle, Arkansas
chapters.summary: Young describes co-founding the Black United Youth in Little Rock, Arkansas around 1968-1971 with Bobby Brown, Bob Broadway (a Philander Smith College graduate and KOKY-AM news announcer working under Larry Hayes), and others. BUY focused on Black student representation in newly desegregated colleges and high schools. Young recounts being in Earle, Arkansas the night school authorities closed the Black high school and forced students into the white one, a state trooper terrorized residents and beat an elderly woman with a walking cane; Young drove her group back to Little Rock, Arkansas in a Mustang and John W. Walker called out the National Guard.
related_events: Black student-union organizing in Arkansas universities

chapters.title: Political Analysis and Obama Reflections
chapters.summary: Young reflects in 2011 on what changed and what did not after the 1960s, economic access for Black businesses remained limited; she perceived a shift in white drug-law treatment after Vietnam veterans returned addicted; she cites the September 2011 execution of Troy Davis as evidence the country is not progressing. She discusses her support for President Obama, the unprecedented demand that he produce a resume and birth certificate, and what she perceived as muted Black-Little-Rock enthusiasm during the 2008 primary.

chapters.title: Malcolm X Liberation University and Closing
chapters.summary: Young describes her 1971 year at Malcolm X Liberation University in Durham, North Carolina, studying African ethnic groups and martial arts, and leaving after community members objected to her white husband's holiday visit. She closes by characterizing Worth Long as a sweet, gentle man who "danced to the sound of a different beat" and visited her parents whenever in West Helena, Arkansas, and by thanking her Modern Woodmen of America director Albert T. Hurst Jr. for giving her time off to do the interview.

loc_video.caption: Carrie M. Young oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Little Rock, Arkansas, 2011 September 26
